The Teacher stands before us not as a cheerleader but as a witness. He is Solomon, the wisest king Israel ever had, and he opens his reflection with a verdict that cuts across every human ambition: everything is futile. Not some things. Not bad things done badly. Everything—your labor, your accomplishments, your careful plans. The word "futility" appears again and again because the Teacher wants you to feel the weight of it, to stop rationalizing and face what he has seen. He watched generations rise and fall while the earth remained. He observed the sun rising and setting in endless repetition, the wind circling without progress, rivers flowing into an ocean that never fills. These are not poetic musings. They are observations from someone who had every advantage—wealth, wisdom, power—and found that none of it changes the fundamental reality: all human effort under the sun amounts to chasing wind.

What makes this opening so unsettling is that the Teacher does not blame sin or moral failure. He blames the human condition itself. We are creatures who toil and labor, yet we gain nothing that lasts. Our eyes are never satisfied with seeing, our ears never content with hearing. We want more, but more of the same thing only deepens the weariness. Even worse, nothing is truly new. What you think you have discovered, what you believe you have accomplished—it has been done before, and it will be done again. Those who came before you are forgotten. You too will be forgotten. This is not pessimism masquerading as wisdom; it is clarity about what happens when you live solely "under the sun," in the realm of human observation and human control.

The Teacher then shares his credentials: he pursued wisdom itself, accumulating knowledge beyond anyone before him. Yet this pursuit—this supposedly noble quest for understanding—he names it plainly as "a miserable task God has laid upon" humanity. He did not fail to find answers. Rather, the more he learned, the more sorrow came with it. Wisdom brought grief. Knowledge increased pain. This is crucial: the problem is not ignorance but knowledge itself. When you truly see how much is wrong, how much cannot be fixed, how much escapes our power to straighten or count—that seeing is painful. The Teacher is inviting you to consider whether the life you are building, the knowledge you are pursuing, the reputation you are chasing, actually leads anywhere worth going.

Yet the Teacher speaks these words intentionally, preserving them for us to read. He did not end his life in despair or silence, nor does he tell you to stop thinking or working. Instead, he forces you to confront an uncomfortable question: if everything under the sun is futile, where does meaning actually come from? He is not giving you comfort. He is giving you clarity about where comfort cannot be found—in human achievement, accumulation, or even wisdom divorced from God. Only by facing the futility of "under the sun" can you begin to look toward something beyond it, toward what endures when all else crumbles. The Teacher's opening verdict is not the end of the story; it is an invitation to think more deeply about what truly matters.
